name: Severe Hyperkalaemia
identifyBy: Progressive ECG changes as K+ rises: peaked T → wide QRS → PR prolongation → P wave flattening → sine wave
Sine wave pattern (sinusoidal QRS-T morphology) = pre-terminal - immediate risk of arrest
No discernible P waves when severely elevated (>7.0 mmol/L)
Often associated with renal failure, DKA, crush injury, rhabdomyolysis

measurements.stSegment: Merges with T wave in sine wave pattern

management: Calcium gluconate 10mL 10% IV over 2–5 minutes - membrane stabilisation (acts in 1–2 min)
Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50mL IV - shifts K+ intracellularly (metabolic acidosis)
Salbutamol 10–20mg nebulised - beta-2 mediated K+ shift into cells
Dextrose/Insulin (if available) - insulin drives K+ into cells
Urgent evacuation for dialysis if renal failure
If arrest occurs: treat cause alongside standard ALS
pearls: Calcium gluconate does NOT lower serum K+ - it stabilises the cardiac membrane to buy time
The ECG changes correlate imperfectly with K+ level - always treat the ECG, not just the number
Sine wave pattern + no pulse = treat as PEA (reversible cause: H = hyperkalaemia)
Pseudo-hyperkalaemia: haemolysed sample - repeat before treating if patient clinically stable
ECG may normalise rapidly with treatment - repeat ECG every 30 minutes after interventions
differentials: Drug toxicity (sodium channel blockers - e.g. TCA overdose)

qaNotes: Electrolyte pattern emphasises QT/ST duration and T-wave morphology.
